exemplar.title: Exploring Lagrangian Constrained Optimization to find the Optimal Dimensions of Composite and Non-Composite Drink Can Designs

detailedExemplarData.title: Exploring Lagrangian Constrained Optimization to find the Optimal Dimensions of Composite and Non-Composite Drink Can Designs

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear table of contents and numbered sections guide the reader effectively
Well-structured narrative with distinct introduction, body and evaluation
Logical progression from context through modelling to solution
weaknesses: Conclusion is embedded within the evaluation rather than as a separate closing section
Occasional subsection ordering issues (e.g., duplicated constraint lines)
Verbose background and large calculator screenshots interrupt focus
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration is coherent and logically developed (band 3) with a clear structure and effective use of headings. While the table of contents is comprehensive and the argument flows well, minor ordering inconsistencies and a lack of a standalone concluding section prevent a topâband award. The work is mostly relevant but includes some unnecessary elements, aligning with a final score of 3/4.

strengths: Consistent and mostly accurate use of standard notation (Ï, r, h, Î») throughout
Effective use of multiple representations (formula tables, shape nets, labelled figures)
Good logical structure with clear stepâbyâstep derivations and accompanying prose
weaknesses: Minor lapses in notation (missing parentheses in Lagrangian, occasional algebraic slips)
Absence of a graph or Hessian test limits the variety of representations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Mathematical language is used mostly consistently, and different representations are employed effectively. The logical flow is clear throughout. These qualities meet the band 4 descriptors, despite a few notational errors and missed opportunities for additional graphical analysis, resulting in a score of 4/4.

strengths: Investigation framed around the studentâs future cafÃ© business shows strong personal linkage
Independent derivation of formulae and self-taught application of Lagrange multipliers demonstrate high initiative
Survey and shape-selection process add a clear personal dimension
weaknesses: Mathematical approach follows standard textbook procedure rather than introducing novel algorithms
No development of a digital tool or original model refinement beyond syllabus techniques
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student displays outstanding personal engagement through a clearly individual research question, independent learning of advanced techniques, and a bespoke survey process tied to real-world interests. Although the core mathematics is classical, the initiative and personal connection merit the top band score of 3/3.

strengths: Meaningful reflection on modelling assumptions (shape idealization, zero thickness)
Thoughtful discussion comparing optimums for different can designs and economic interpretation of Î»
weaknesses: Limited quantitative evaluation of real-world viability (e.g., sensitivity analysis)
Evaluation of calculator precision and Hessian test is noted only as a future idea without depth
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Reflection is meaningful and threaded throughout the work, with thoughtful analysis of assumptions and outcomes. However, it lacks substantial critical depth and systematic evaluation of limitations or future implications, aligning with band 2. A score of 2/3 captures these strengths and areas for development.

strengths: Comprehensive application of relevant mathematics (surfaceâarea formulae, constraint equations, Lagrangian formulation)
Thorough understanding of multivariable calculus at SL level with correct substitution and solution of partial derivatives
Economic interpretation of the multiplier Î» demonstrates deep conceptual insight
weaknesses: No secondâderivative (Hessian) test to confirm minima
A few minor algebraic omissions and notational inconsistencies in composite-shape derivations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Mathematics is applied comprehensively to both can designs, fully supporting the research question. The work goes beyond basic requirements by integrating advanced calculus, though it stops short of employing a Hessian test. These attributes align with band 5, yielding a score of 5/6.

strandSelections.reasoning: Step-by-step derivations are set out logically, and prose explanations accompany the algebra, enabling a reader at SL level to understand the process.  Occasional algebraic slips (e.g. missing minus sign when expanding the Lagrangian for the composite can) are later corrected, but the narrative remains clear, meeting the âgood logical structure and clear communicationâ descriptor.

Derivatives and algebraic manipulation are largely correct, leading to sensible optimal dimensions.  Minor notational errors and a few algebraic slips (later self-corrected) prevent a perfect score, but overall understanding is thorough and accuracy is high, aligning with band 4.

Equations, a table summarising formulae, labelled figures of the shapes, a net of a cylinder and a calculator screenshot are provided.  These multiple representations help the reader visualise the models and follow the algebra.  The absence of graphs (e.g. SA vs r) and of a Hessian confirmation limits the variety, so the work aligns with âeffectiveâ rather than âhighly effectiveâ use.

The exploration follows a clear narrative: introduction of the business context, derivation of formulae, construction of the Lagrangian, differentiation, solution and evaluation. Each section flows sensibly into the next and the argument is easy to follow. However, occasional digressions (e.g. survey discussion, lengthy calculator screen shots) interrupt the flow, and some passages repeat earlier explanations of Lagrange multipliers. These minor breaks prevent the work from reaching the âhighly coherent / excellent logical developmentâ descriptor.

Learning multivariable calculus independently and simplifying a composite-shape problem exhibits creativity and initiative beyond the syllabus.  Yet the mathematical techniques finally used are classical and no original algorithm or digital tool is developed, so the strand fits the âgood levelâ rather than âexceptionalâ band.

There is thoughtful discussion comparing the two shapes, recognising how Î» relates to material-saving, and noting potential consumer preferences.  Nonetheless, analysis stops short of examining sensitivity to volume changes or validating minima with the Hessian, so it is âthoughtfulâ rather than âdeep and insightfulâ.

The student comments generally on using a calculator and proposes employing a Hessian test in future work, but there is little systematic evaluation of accuracy, limitations or implications beyond anecdotal remarks.  Consequently the work shows âlimited evaluationâ rather than a clear or comprehensive one.

The student reflects on modelling assumptions (perfect shapes, zero thickness) and on unexpected width/height outcomes, showing meaningful engagement with results.  Reflection is woven into relevant sections, but depth is limited; the real-world viability of squat cans is not quantitatively analysed.  Thus the descriptor âmeaningful reflection throughoutâ is satisfied, but not the âsubstantial, criticalâ level.

Variables, parameters and constants are generally stated correctly and conventional notation (Ï, r, h, Î») is used through most of the work.  Minor lapses â such as writing $\delta \mathcal L \delta r$ instead of $\partial\mathcal L/\partial r$ and omitting parentheses in several formulae â reduce accuracy, so the use is âmostly consistentâ rather than fully âconsistent and accurateâ.

The investigation is clearly framed around the studentâs future cafÃ© business, and decisions (volume choice, shape selection after a survey) reflect an individual pathway.  This establishes âclear evidence of a personal approachâ, but the approach is not especially novel or innovative to merit the top band.

Most material is directly related to determining optimal can dimensions; however, the survey story, repeated explanations of basic calculus rules and large calculator screenshots add length without advancing the mathematics.  The prose is often verbose (e.g. reiterating why 500 ml was chosen).  Therefore the exploration is âmostly relevant with some unnecessary elementsâ rather than consistently concise.

Surface-area formulae, constraint equations, Lagrangian formation, partial derivatives, and simultaneous solutions are all relevant and integral to answering the research question.  Mathematics is applied comprehensively to both designs and supports all conclusions, matching the descriptor for band 5.

The choice to study two can designs with an uncommon optimisation technique for SL, and the derivation of formulae from first principles, demonstrate notable independence.  The student articulates personal curiosity about cost-saving and sustainability.  However, the mathematics closely follows standard textbook Lagrange-multiplier procedure, so the evidence stops short of âoutstandingâ.

The student supplies a Table of Contents, uses numbered / titled sections and provides a beginning, middle and end.  Headings such as âPartially differentiatingâ¦â, âSetting â¦ = 0â and âResearch Evaluationâ guide the reader effectively.  Nevertheless, sub-sections occasionally appear in an odd order (e.g. two identical constraint lines in the cylinderâhemisphere section) and the final conclusion is embedded within the evaluation rather than offered as a distinct closing section.  Hence the organisation is âwell organised with clear structureâ rather than âexceptionally well organisedâ.

The use of multivariable calculus sits above the SL syllabus and is therefore fully commensurate with course level.  However, the work does not extend into second-derivative tests or numerical optimisation software, so it does not reach the âwell-applied throughoutâ expectation of band 5.

annotations.comment: The Table of Contents is comprehensive but overly detailed, which can overwhelm the reader. Consider streamlining headings and grouping related subsections to improve navigability and conciseness.
The introduction clearly outlines aims and context but is verbose. You could condense some background on sustainable development to focus more sharply on the mathematical objectives.

The Lagrangian function is defined without consistent parentheses around the constraint term, which can lead to confusion. Use $$



definition: \mathcal{L}=f(r,h)-\lambda\,(g(r,h)-C)$$.
The Lagrange function for the composite can on page 9 shows unbalanced parentheses and duplicates the volume term. Verify each component before differentiating.
The substitution step for Î» is executed correctly, showing solid algebraic skill. Well done.
When computing r as a cube root, include unit analysis and show the cube root extraction step explicitly for clarity.
The reference to âFigure Xâ in the text lacks a figure number; ensure that all calculator screenshots are correctly labeled and numbered.
The composite shape derivation on page 14 begins but omits several derivative terms and their combination; include full differentiation steps.
Your discussion of surprising can dimensions touches on consumer preference but could be deepened by analysing sensitivity of surface area to volume or shape variation.
The research question is well-formulated and shows clear personal engagement by linking the exploration to your future cafÃ© business.
The contextual paragraph on standard can dimensions provides useful background but repeats points made earlier. Consider merging or shortening to maintain focus.
The survey process demonstrating your shape-selection reasoning is a clear sign of initiative and gives a personal dimension to the investigation.
Figure captions combine two separate figures in one line and lack numbering clarity. Label each figure individually and add distinct captions.
The explanation around the lateral surface area introduces stray notation â(w Ã y)r)â. Clarify the notation and ensure consistency with the rest of the text.
The net of the cylinder (Figure 3) is well chosen and effectively illustrates the relationship between circumference and lateral surface area.
The composite shape volume derivation mixes narrative and algebra. Present each step as a discrete equation to improve clarity and traceability.

In Table 1, the total surface area formula for the composite shape is incorrect. It should account for one base and hemisphere area: $$A=2\,
Ïr^2+2Ïrh+Ïr^2=3Ïr^2+2Ïrh$$.
Your discussion of modelling assumptions is concise and shows awareness of real-world limitations in thickness and surface imperfections.
Your interpretation of Î»âs economic meaning is insightful and demonstrates strong understanding of the multiplierâs role.

In the derivative of 2ÏrÂ², the first line misstates the power rule. It should directly yield $$

d/d r(2ÏrÂ²)=4Ïr$$.
Numbering your system of equations when setting derivatives to zero will aid reference and discussion in subsequent calculations.
The bibliography lists an online calculator but omits discussion of its precision limits. Reflect on potential errors introduced by the tool.
The research evaluation acknowledges Hessian matrix use in future work, demonstrating reflection. Consider explaining why this is essential for confirming minima.
The Research Evaluation heading marks a clear structure but consider distinguishing the conclusion as a separate section for better organization.
